speaker: interviewer
text: --me about you smuggled yourself into Theresienstadt on a number of occasions. How many were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I went-- I went-- [CLEARS THROAT] --nine times. And I don't know if it's the third time, fourth time, I don't know. [COUGHS] Goldie gave me a spool of film. He took his, his photo of-- like, or whatever he had. He took everything to Theresienstadt. And he said, it was the first hanging in the ghetto, in the yard of the-- of the army barracks. They put up that scaffolding. They hung-- I, I don't know-- two, three, four. I can't remember. And they put it where the barrels were with the soup. And when the people came to-- the, the boys who came for their breakfast or for their lunch, they had their friends hanging up there. And he had-- he knew what is going to happen when they were putting up the scaffolding. So he went on the roof of the army barrack. There was three floors high. And he took the whole film of the hanging. And he gave me that film. I mean, I-- it was crazy to give me that film and for me to, to take it. I mean, they would have killed me if they would have caught me with that film. Anyhow, you know, it was very important to me. I had no resistance to take the film, so I took the film. And he gave me a name and an address. It was somebody Czech, a photographer. And he was to develop it, and then he was to do something with it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I did what Goldie asked me, and I never heard about it. I never heard if it was sent overseas, or if anybody saw it. I, I don't know happened with the film, but I certainly took it out. And do you know why the boys were hung?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why they what?

speaker: interviewer
text: Why the boys were hung, do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Look, when, when I met Goldie, if, if we were together for 15 minutes, it was the most. Because I-- it was more dangerous, in a way, for me, because I went into the ghetto, but I also had to get out of the ghetto and back to that hotel. So he didn't want me, you know, to hang around too long, because he wanted me to get back into safety. I mean, nobody was walking around in Theresienstadt in the middle of the night. So it was pretty obvious when, when I was walking from the Jewish part into the-- into the German hotel. It, it wasn't very pleasant.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I-- we never-- it wasn't nice meetings, happy meeting. They were under so, so much pressure and under so much fear always. What did you see in Theresienstadt?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, nothing. It, it was a-- as I said, a Garrison town where they were before soldiers. And, and now they were- they were Jewish people. And they were Czech people living in, in little houses. I mean, if you-- if you wouldn't have known, there was nothing abnormal to see at, at that time. The street that, that went, that was like a normal road if you today go into some village or something. There was nothing to see. What I knew is what-- that they didn't have much to eat, that, that they cut their hair, and that they had to work very hard. But they were all young guys, and they didn't mind, because they didn't think-- that was OK. They were two hours from Prague. I mean, that didn't seem so dramatic. OK, they didn't have the freedom, but they were alive. And if they didn't do something very bad, they obviously were not threatened or in danger.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a lot of information about what was going on in other parts, say with Poland for instance?

speaker: interviewee
text: We did not know about Poland. We did not know about Auschwitz. We did not know about anything. All we knew that the Jews are going to be settled in, in like a ghetto-like place, Theresienstadt. Which didn't seem-- it seemed bad, of course, but it didn't seem that bad. Nobody knew about Poland at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hm. Tell me how it came about the Goldie was caught.

speaker: interviewee
text: After the eighth visit, somebody-- again, one of those girls-- came to me and said they changed, because somehow they didn't want to use the [? Bauhoff ?] anymore-- maybe there was some trouble-- and that Goldie had made contact with a Czech family. He was very charming, and his Czech was very good. And, and so he made contact with them. And he asked them if they would permit that I meet him there, and they said, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I got the address of one of those little house and the time when I'm supposed to be there. And I was to bring money, and if-- probably salami, and nougat, and some other more essential things. So again, I, I had the two zipper bags. And I went-- I went to Theresienstadt. I went into the hotel. And when the time came, which was again, at night, I went, and I knocked there. The people opened and told that Goldie is upstairs. It was a little two0story house.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went upstairs. And again, we exchanged-- you know, talking about the most important things. And I gave him all, all, all what I brought. And again, we, we didn't make any arrangements, because I was again to get word through somebody where to meet, because they didn't know if the Czech families could have been already evacuated. Because slowly, slowly, they were moving all, all the the non-Jewish people out. And I went back home. And I went to the mother, and I told her that I have seen Goldie, that everything is fine. And about two days later, a, a Czech girl came to me, and my Czech was so poor. And I knew it was something terribly bad she's, she's telling me. And eventually, I worked it out with her that he had a cart. And he had an after- hour pass, which I don't know what they had it for-- maybe if somebody died that they, they carried the bodies at night. I really don't know. Anyhow, when I left all the luggage there, he put it in his little cart. And he had his after-hour pass. And off he went to where he was living. And a Czech gendarme or sort of a country policemen-- a Czech, mind you-- caught him and put him in with the German authorities. Then all I know, what I've been told, he went to the small fortress. He was-- that was terrible. They beat them there. They tortured them. And knowing that, that my visit caused all this disaster-- I mean, sure, it wasn't my idea. But it was his idea, but I cooperated. And my cooperation caused all this. I just was frantic. And there was no way of, of getting any, any-- even if people went to the ghetto, they still didn't know what's going on in, in the fortress. So that was in, in-- he was deported on the 9th of May, 1942, address unknown. So we didn't know where the people went.

speaker: interviewer
text: And for one year, I, I just-- I just was going nuts. I just wanted to know what did I do? What happened? What happened to him? So eventually, I had this terribly crazy idea that what if I go to the Jewish Council and ask them, because I'm half Jewish, why can't I be in the transport? Because a lot of half Jewish people were there, only that they had Jewish religion. I didn't have, so big deal. What's the difference? So that if-- if they could put me in the transport? And they thought I'm a naughty teenager, you know, and they hardly even talked to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: But there was one very nice man, a young man. And he sort of understood a little bit my torment. And he said, look, they only thing you can do is if you convert to Judaism, then we can transport you. And so he thought, oh well, I got rid of her. That's OK. And I got the address of a rabbi. And I had to go really for real, to write a bit in Hebrew, and to say a prayer, and to learn the customs of, of Jewish women, what to do and what not to do, which all didn't interest me at all. And when the rabbi finally was satisfied, I went and had the mikveh. And after I was in the mikveh, I went to the authorities. I had to go to the Czech authorities to say that I am no longer Evangelic, that I am now a Jew. And they put it in some big book. And then I went to Jewish Council. And I said, here you are. Now I am a Jew. Now deport me. And exactly one year after Goldie disappeared, on the 19th of May, 1943, I went to Theresienstadt.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- where was your father at this stage?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was deported about half a year after Goldie, which was just in between when, when I, I was already half crazy about Goldie, then my poor father went to Theresienstadt. Then I couldn't get any-- I couldn't-- I didn't know what happened to my father, what happened to Goldie. Somehow after I think six months, they've become much worse. The more Czech people were moved out, the harder it was to get into the ghetto. So I think that we had no contact at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: So once I came to Theresienstadt, of course, my father wasn't there. Of course, Goldie wasn't there. And nobody, nobody could help me with any information at all. What did your mother think about your conversion?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my mother felt terrible for me, and I think I must have driven her crazy. Because she, she saw the first time how vulnerable I am, because as rule, I'm not. And, and with this Goldie situation, she knew that, that she can't stop me. And because I was only-- I was under-- I was-- how old was I in, in '40-- I was-- I was underage when, when I did all that. For the mikveh and all that, I didn't need to be of age. But I needed-- when I was deported, I thought it would be good if I would be like, no, no longer under age. So we went to some department somewhere, I don't know. And there was a piece of paper that she signed that I am of age, and that I can do what I want. And I thought that might protect her if, you know, heaven knows what they will do next to my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, was there any danger for her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, of course, it, it, it couldn't have been very nice. But, but you see, I just disappeared. So when people asked where I am-- and a lot of people were sent to Germany, went to work, Czech people. So she says, Senta is in Germany working. She didn't tell anybody that I volunteered to, to go into the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your mother survive after your father went?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I mean, they have been married for such a long time. And I think it, it, it wasn't always very happy going. So I think that she felt very sorry and very sad, but I don't think it, it was that she couldn't live without him. It wasn't, you know, like some people are so madly in love after 40 years or so. That wasn't the case at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember anything that your father said to you when he left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, he left me a letter. And when I escaped in 1948 from Czechoslovakia when the communists took over, I escape with a tiny little suitcase and a lot of things that were very precious to me, like one card I got from Goldie when he was going from Theresienstadt, the letter from my father. All the negatives Goldie did, I wanted to keep for him. I left with somebody. And I never went back to Czechoslovakia. And the person-- I mean, I did go back after the war. But the person just disappeared.

speaker: interviewer
text: So do you remember the contents of the letter?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I think a lot of things I try to block out, because they are just too hurtful.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: I felt terrible about-- I felt also guilty about my father. I thought maybe I should have talked my parents out of getting a divorce.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hm. Did you notice a change in the attitude of people towards you once you became Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were all Jews in the ghetto. It was lovely.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, before you went into the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nobody knew I was Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, nobody knew about our family, because it was a mixed family. We were not a religious family running to the temple or anything. Nobody-- they thought more that we are Germans. You know, the Czechs never distinguished between Austrians and Germans. And because my parents were Viennese, and we always talked German, so to the Czechs, we were Germans, never Jews, you know, till--

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me about your deportation to Theresienstadt.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was so excited. And I remembered that in, in Goldie's flat-- in the-- in the meantime, his parents were also deported that year that I tried, you know, to work out what I am to do. And I still had the key, and the place was still empty.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I remembered that we had to have black curtains for-- because of, of air-- what is it called? Well, at night, the whole city had to be black. And I remembered they were black felt curtains. So I went back into the flat, and I pulled them down. And I had a sleeping bag made out of the black curtains. Worst thing, because when you have fleas in a sleeping bag, it's the worst thing that can happen. Anyhow, I had that sleeping bag made.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, and I prepared for-- I was so excited like if I would go to the Riviera, you know, that I'm on the 19th of May going to Theresienstadt. And we were permitted, I don't know, 80, 80 kilos of, of-- that's quite a lot of stuff.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when I came to the-- to Theresienstadt, the, the train was already going through the-- through the city. So we were brought right into, into the middle of the city. And I don't know if we were 500 or 1,000. I have no idea. And people were always coming to have a look, who is coming again, you know, from Prague? And they were all Goldie's friends, girls I knew. And they says, why are you here? Why are you-- is the war finishing? You know, I don't know why they thought when I come to Theresienstadt, that the war is finishing. So I found so many of, of our friends there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And once I was there, actually, I felt much better. Because I knew that I'm sharing part of-- it wasn't bad at all for me. It was much better when I came then when he was there, because he had to prepare. Once-- there were about 60,000 people by the time I,I came to the ghetto, maybe less. I don't know. But it was-- it wasn't bad for young people. It wasn't bad. I mean, if you worked, you had a little bit-- a bit bigger ration to eat. And also, my mother was very kind. You could send parcels from Prague-- food parcels. Not so many-- you, you got a certificate, I don't know, once a month, or once in three months, or something. And then somebody could send you a food parcel.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, so she-- she did. I don't know where she got it. She must have gotten it all on the black market. So I mean, I was certainly not living in misery.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, I had fleas. I had-- I had no freedom to, to go back to Prague. And looking back at Theresienstadt, after Auschwitz and the other camps, it just wasn't bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the Jews perceive you?

speaker: interviewee
text: How did they what?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the Jewish people in Theresienstadt-- how did they perceive you when you arrived there and--

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- look, I didn't know any old people. All I knew is young people. And the young people, they thought it was terrific what I did. Because they knew somehow that I had been going there, and I belonged totally. I mean, they never thought of me like a German, or a half Jew, or something. That was-- we were a clique of young people who mixed. It was like a family. And, and I felt-- I felt much better being there than in Prague, really.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you living?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was living first in, in the Hamburger caserne up right under the roof. That was terribly hot and very, very-- and fleas. God, the fleas were so awful. You couldn't sleep or anything. And later on there, they built some, some barracks a little bit outside of Theresienstadt. It was already so full in Theresienstadt that they had to, to build more. And it was called West [? Barrack. ?] And, and it was all new, brand new. And so I, with my girlfriend, Dita-- who, who lives now in, in Spain-- we moved to the West [? Barrack. ?] It was very-- friendship was the most important thing in the camps, to have people who you care for. And so we lived in the West [? Barrack. ?] And I had a good job. I was in the bath, bath house. And women, we came-- we came once a week. They got little docket, I don't know if once a week. And they came there. And for that little docket, they could have a hot shower. And I was manipulating the showers. And I had a certain time to do it. I mean, I wasn't permitted to let the hot water on forever. And I remember, I don't know, five minutes or three minutes-- and when I turned the hot water off, they were all screaming at me. And so I had a good job. I could wash things in, in hot water. There was even a small swimming pool. That was all from the German soldiers. They had their showers, and their swimming pool. So I had a swimming pool. And only then, I got diphtheria. I was really, very, very, very sick. And I got out of it again, and, and functioned again.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the news of Goldie? Did you find out--

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing, absolutely nothing, nothing, nothing, nothing. And then, after-- well, I was in Theresienstadt not quite a year and a half. And slowly, slowly, the-- transport, after transport, after transport. First, it was old people. Then it was mixed, and then, all at once, a lot of young men. And what they left-- or what were mostly half Jewish people, which I was, again, in it. And they were, again, protected. Because when you were half Jewish in Theresienstadt, you stayed right to the end in the camp. You didn't go away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So all my girlfriends, funnily-- it wasn't by choice, by coincidence-- they were all half Jewish. And they all had their boyfriends deported. So what we did, next transport came-- they did a different things all at once before they put things in the-- people in the transport, the people didn't want to go. There was a lot of commotion.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they said, OK. It's easier that the Jews did all that. Only the Germans wanted a number. And they didn't care, you know, who went or who didn't go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Jewish Council said, we take also volunteers. So the whole group of us, from what I know, at least 20 women, we all volunteered to go to find our men.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had the stupid idea that we are all going to Germany, and at there will be the boys, you know. And it will be wonderful. So we got in the train. And three days later, we came-- oh, it, it was going on too long, the trip. And the countryside looked horrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was September. It was cold. It was-- oh, it was spooky, this whole end of the trip. And then all at once, we stopped. And then those guys in the-- in the striped pajamas with their shaven heads, the little caps, came opening the doors and screaming, out, out, out. Leave, leave the luggage, you know? And then I looked. And I saw this arbeit macht frei, work frees you, and the wires, electric wires, and all this. And I said, what have I done? Well, once I was there, I couldn't do anything. So I took a loaf of bread and a tin of pate out of my luggage. Where exactly were you? [COUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: In Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: They got-- they gave us supplies before we left. They gave us some supplies. And I kept the loaf of bread and the pate. And I thought, oh well, you know, I better-- I better have something to eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then we were hoarded into, into, into five, five, five, five. And I, I was so well dressed. I had ski shoes. I had warm slacks. I had everything warm. And it was so cold. I said, just as well, you know? I'm so practical. I'm dressed so well.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we went-- we must have gone before-- I, I don't know when Mengele was or when I dropped the bread. I must have dropped the bread and the pate before Mengele. I know it was a terribly dusty road-- not dusty, muddy road. And we were walking on that road.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the women from the barracks were screaming at us, throw us your, your watches and your jewelry. And I, I wouldn't have thrown anything. I mean, what would they have done with it? Actually, they could do-- I think there was a lot of black market going on in Auschwitz. But I didn't want to do anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the guard-- one of the guards that was walking alongside shouted at the woman. And the woman didn't go into the barrack as he asked her, and so he shot her. And it was the first time I saw somebody being shot. And she fell down. In mid September, she had only a little smock on. She was naked underneath. And as she fell, the whole smock fell away. And there she was naked. I got such a scare, I dropped my bread and my pate. Because I thought. I better not break the rules in this place. And then I think we came to, to, where, where this table was. And on it was this SS man. And we were five, five, fives. But then, before we came to the table, we had to go one by one. And I noticed my girlfriend, one of my girlfriends-- it was a very beautiful Dutch girl, Helen Van Leven, she was called. And she also went in the transport as a volunteer, because she had fallen in love with a Czech guy. And she also wanted to find her Czech guy. And she was standing near a woman with two little children. And the woman had one child up, and the second was running around, and the woman was struggling. And Helen picked up the child and held the child. I was very aware of it. I can't say why. Anyhow, when my turn came to, to come to that guy with the polished boots, and black gloves, and SSS, and he said, left right, left right, I only know that I was on the other side than my Dutch girlfriend that was still holding the child. And somehow, I had the feeling that looking at us that were the one in my group, I had the feeling that I'm in the right group, because they were young. They were looking healthy. And on the other one, there were some older people and children. And I had a feeling that, that she, she did something awful picking up that child. She was killed the same day. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the registration.

speaker: interviewee
text: About what?

speaker: interviewer
text: The registration.

speaker: interviewee
text: Registration?

speaker: interviewer
text: When you went into Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, when we went into Auschwitz. Well, once we-- once we went past Mengele and, and, you know, again, five, five, five, we walk, walk, walk. And then, of course, we came to the shower room. And by that time, of course, all our luggage was left in the train. I already had the feeling I'll never see my things again. And then we were again hoarded into some big hall and told to, to watch out that we leave every-- all our clothes together, that we will find it easily.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when I-- when I undressed, I had a little photo of Goldie and a silver Buddha. And I thought, no, I'd-- I better take that with me, you know? So I went into the showers with all my other friends, and we didn't know that the showers can be gas chamber. So we were not surprised when the water came, because we didn't know about anything else. Nobody knew about Auschwitz. So after we were showered, we never went back where our clothes was. We went out on the other side naked in September, wet, naked.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we were given little-- one piece of cloth like, let's say, like a waitress uniform or something and those Dutch shoes that the Dutch people, the country people, wear. And then we were hoarded into this big barrack. And it had the three- tier beds. Oh, no, no, no-- before, our hair was cut. Before we went into the accommodation, we, we were again standing. And they were Slovakian girls. And they were-- the were cutting our hair. I was so angry. I had this long hair. And in front of me was, again, a beautiful Hungarian woman-- black hair, blue eyes. She was so beautiful. Didn't look Jewish at all, not that Jews-- Jews are also very beautiful. But-- and she was-- when the woman came with the clippers, this elegant, gorgeous woman took off a wig and put it down. [LAUGHTER] And I just couldn't believe it. So then later, many-- after nearly at the end of the war, I ask her about the wig. I just couldn't believe it that she was so religious that she wore a wig.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyhow, they cut all-- my all pubic, under the arm, and our hair off. And then, then we went into the barracks. And it was so cold. And we were 10 on one of those wooden things. So the first and the last was always freezing. So we, we swapped all night who is the first and went inside. And when we wanted to turn, it was so narrow, we had to be on our side. And we all had to turn in-- on the same moment. And the food we got was in, in cheap wash basins, one basin for 10 women. And here we were, dirty, full of lice. And we had to put the hands in the wash basin to eat. And some couldn't do it. I did it. Because I, I thought, if I'm here, I'm going to get out of it. I just don't want to stay here. And because I was right on top, there were-- it was like horse barracks that had little slits on, on top where you could look out. And one day, there was an awful commotion, and, and I, I looked out. And there was a soldier. And he was-- very thin women were running in front of him. And, and he was behind them. And obviously, he wanted to take them to the gas chambers. And they were running all over the place, and he was shooting and screaming. And by that time, of course, we knew everything. We knew about the gas chambers. And the smell of the-- of the chimney was just unbelievable. It, it just-- it's-- the stench is just awful. And at night, you saw the flames coming out.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll stop there.
